reuse: No explicit copyright/license/reuse statement was found on the Specifications landing page or in accompanying order-form/GASP documents during this research. As a public Alabama state agency publication, it is presumptively public-record material, but ALDOT does not post a stated open-reuse or Creative Commons license the way some states do; a physical printed copy can also be purchased ($30 + shipping) via an order form, suggesting the state does not treat wide free redistribution as a settled/promoted use case.
notes: Primary domain is dot.state.al.us; a mirrored/newer domain aldot.gov also serves identical PDF paths (e.g. https://www.aldot.gov/publications/Construction/pdf/Specifications/2026/2026_SPEC_BOOK_FINAL_PDF_COPY_11-19-25.pdf) and may become the canonical domain going forward. The current 2026 edition PDF was fetched directly and confirmed live (200 OK, application/pdf, ~10.1MB). Interim amendments between full-book editions are published individually as "General Application Special Provisions" (GASP) PDFs under each edition's folder (e.g. pdf/Specifications/2026/26-GA0001.pdf), which is useful context for a section-history feature since GASPs show incremental section-level changes between the 4-year(ish) full-book cycles. The ALDOT Letting/bidding portal (alletting.dot.state.al.us) hosts Standard Drawings separately from the Specifications book. No login/portal is required to access any of the specification PDFs — all are open, direct-linked files. Section numbering was confirmed via search snippets referencing Section 105 (Control of Work), Section 106 (Control of Materials), Section 107 (Legal Relations), and Section 501-503 (structural concrete/steel/foundations) with Article-level decimals like 105.02/105.03, matching the AASHTO-derived standard spec format used by most state DOTs (distinct from Washington's Division-dash-Section '1-09.7' scheme).

verification.observation:
The specs page (dot.state.al.us/publications/Construction/Specifications.html) loads fine and links to the 2026 Standard Specifications, and the primary PDF URL (2026_SPEC_BOOK_FINAL_PDF_COPY_11-19-25.pdf) resolves 200 OK and downloads a real 826-page ALDOT document titled "Standard Specifications for Highway Construction, 2026 Edition," with an ALDOT Montgomery, AL address on the copyright/purchase page. So the URL, single-PDF format, and "2026 Edition" claims all check out.

However, the numbering/example claim is wrong on the key detail. Section 105 is indeed titled "CONTROL OF WORK" and Section 501 is indeed "STRUCTURAL PORTLAND CEMENT CONCRETE" (Division 500 - Structures), so those top-level section titles match. But the specific worked example is incorrect: Article 105.03 in the actual 2026 book is titled "Conformity with Plans and Specifications," not "Cooperation by Contractor." I searched the extracted full text of Section 105 and could not find the phrase "Cooperation by Contractor" anywhere near Section 105 (in fact a text search for that exact phrase returned zero hits in the pages I checked). The article numbering style is also worth flagging: it is single/double-digit decimal without leading zeros (e.g. 105.1... up through 105.10, 105.12, and specifically "105.03," "105.05" appear with a leading zero only for single-digit articles - the pattern is inconsistent enough that the researcher's clean "105.02, 105.03" framing overstates uniformity). The AASHTO-style Section+Article description and Division-100/-500 structure is directionally correct, but the specific citation used to illustrate it (105.03 = "Cooperation by Contractor") is fabricated or misattributed - it should be something like "Conformity with Plans and Specifications." I could not independently verify the "effective 01/01/2026, used beginning January 30, 2026 letting" administrative detail from the PDF content itself (no explicit effective-date/letting language was found on the cover or foreword pages I reviewed), so that claim is unverified, not confirmed.

Note: during automated extraction I twice observed anomalous/corrupted downloads of the same URL (once yielding what looked like Iowa DOT content and a different page count) before a clean re-download produced consistent, verifiable ALDOT content - this appears to be a transient fetch/caching artifact in my tooling rather than a server-side issue, since the final clean copy matches the known ALDOT specs page and title.

A 2015 edition PDF exists at the same predictable URL pattern (sshc2015.pdf returns HTTP 200) but is not linked anywhere on the page itself (only indirectly implied via a 'changesfrom2015.pdf' summary doc) - it is an orphan file, not part of a published archive. Editions further back (2004, 2008, 2012) return 404 at that path; a 2004 edition circulates only via third-party mirrors (e.g. Scribd) and old municipal bid-document reprints, not an official DOT&PF archive. Net effect: unlike WSDOT's deep browsable back-catalog, Alaska DOT&PF exposes at most 2-3 editions, none older than 2015, through official channels.

reuse: No explicit copyright, license, or reuse statement was found in the front matter of the 2020 Standard Specifications (checked cover, questions/comments, and preface pages). It states only that the document is "published only in an electronic format" and "may be viewed or downloaded at no charge" from the DOT&PF website. As a State of Alaska government work it is presumably free to reproduce/reuse absent an explicit restriction, but this is inferred, not stated on the record.
notes: Key difference from Washington: WSDOT publishes a long, easily browsable back-catalog of editions, but Alaska DOT&PF's public specs page (https://dot.alaska.gov/stwddes/dcsspecs/) currently exposes only the 2020 (current, 3.3MB) and 2017 (previous, 2.7MB) editions as linked single-PDF downloads covering the whole book (Divisions 100 through at least 700+), plus separate "changes from previous edition" and errata PDFs and separate Standard Modifications / Special Provisions documents layered per edition. A 6x9-inch pocket-format PDF of the current edition is also offered (sshc6x9.pdf). Airport construction has its own parallel Standard Specifications document (also 2020 edition) with a different Part/Division numbering convention (e.g. "Part I General Contract Provisions"). Section numbering within the highway specs resembles Washington's but uses a hyphen instead of a leading division digit: "<3-digit section>-<part>.<item>" (e.g. 401-3.11 Preparation of Asphalt, 105-1.16 Final Acceptance and Record Retention), grouped into Divisions 100 (General Provisions) through higher hundreds (400 = Bases and Pavements, etc.). Because only 2 editions (arguably 3, counting the undocumented 2015 orphan PDF) are realistically reachable through official channels, DOTcompass's flagship cross-edition section-history feature would have much shallower depth for Alaska than for Washington unless additional back issues are sourced from third parties (e.g. municipal bid-document archives like hainesalaska.gov, or Scribd mirrors of a 2004 edition) - those are NOT official DOT&PF sources and carry provenance/reliability risk if used.

notes: Structure: ADOT publishes ONE current base document (the 2021 Standard Specifications for Road and Bridge Construction, ~single large PDF, offered in separate \"PC\" and \"Tablet\" formatted versions of the same content) plus a stream of \"Stored Specifications\" — individual amendment/supplement documents (Word .doc/.docx in a self-extracting zip, plus PDF) issued monthly/bimonthly that modify or supersede specific sections until the next full edition is published. This is analogous to WSDOT's GSP/amendment model but ADOT calls them Stored Specifications. The companion Construction Manual is explicitly organized to mirror the Standard Specifications' chapter/section numbers (e.g., Construction Manual Section 101 = Spec Section 101), which is a useful cross-reference structure for a history tool. Historical editions of the base spec exist back to at least 1969 per the Arizona Memory Project (a state library digitization project, not azdot.gov itself), with only 2021 and 2008 confirmed hosted directly on azdot.gov. A DOTcompass \"history across editions\" feature would need to pull the pre-2021 archive from azmemory.azlibrary.gov rather than azdot.gov alone, and would need to reconstruct effective-date history from the Stored Specifications supplements layered on top of the 2021 base text, since there is no indication ADOT re-publishes an entire consolidated edition between 2008 and 2021 and 2021-present.

numberingScheme: AASHTO-style hierarchical scheme: Division (hundreds block, e.g. Division 100 - General Provisions) contains Sections (e.g. SECTION 101 DEFINITIONS AND TERMS), which contain numbered subsections in Division.Section.sub format (e.g. 101.01, 103.08), which contain lettered/numbered sub-points (a), (b), (1), (2). Direct HTTPS fetch of ardot.gov pages returns HTTP 403 to automated fetchers (site likely blocks non-browser UAs / bot protection), but content was retrieved via a read-only rendering proxy and cross-confirmed by multiple search results, so confidence remains high despite that friction. verification.observation:
The primary spec URL (https://media.ark.org/ardot/2014SpecBook.pdf) resolves with HTTP 200, no login required, returning a real 4.27MB, 890-page PDF titled "Standard Specifications for [Highway Construction]" produced in Adobe Acrobat (CreationDate 2016-10-06). The claimed AASHTO-style numbering scheme checks out exactly against the extracted text: the table of contents shows "DIVISION 100 GENERAL PROVISIONS" containing "SECTION 101 DEFINITION AND TERMS," "SECTION 102 BIDDING REQUIREMENTS AND CONDITIONS," "SECTION 103" (Award/Execution of Contract, with subsection 103.08 explicitly present and confirmed to cover DBE — Disadvantaged Business Enterprise — participation, matching the claimed example verbatim), "SECTION 104 SCOPE OF WORK," "SECTION 106 CONTROL OF MATERIAL" with subsections like 106.01-106.08, "SECTION 108 PROSECUTION AND PROGRESS" with 108.01 etc., and lettered sub-points such as (a) General, (b) Fixed Completion Date, (d) Bidder's Responsibilities, (e) Eligibility of DBEs — all nested exactly as claimed (Division.Section.subsection.letter/number).

numberingScheme: Sections numbered 1-99+ grouped into 12 Roman-numeral Divisions (e.g., Division I General Provisions = Sections 1-9, Division II General Construction = Sections 10-16, Division V Surfacings and Pavements includes Section 39 Asphalt Concrete, Division VI Structures includes Section 51 Concrete Structures, Division IX Traffic Control Devices includes Section 84 Markings). Within a section, subsections use a Section-Subsection.Item hyphenated/dotted format, e.g. "39-2.01" or "59-2.01D(1)", analogous in spirit to WSDOT's "1-09.7" style but with the section number preceding a hyphen rather than a leading division digit.
numberingExample: Section 39-2.01, "Asphalt Concrete — General" (also seen: subsection 59-2.01D(1))

historicalDepth: Publicly downloadable current-and-recent editions on the official page go back to 2018 (editions posted: 2018, 2022, 2023, 2024, 2025 — Caltrans did not necessarily post every intervening year, e.g. 2019-2021 modifications were issued as revisions/digests to the 2018 base rather than full new editions). Editions older than 2018 (e.g. 2010, 2015) are NOT posted on the official site for public download; Caltrans directs requesters to contact their District Local Assistance Engineer (DLAE) for archived copies via a secured file-transfer site. Some older editions (e.g. 2006, 2010) circulate on third-party document-sharing sites (Scribd, Yumpu) but these are not official Caltrans-hosted archive links, so they are not suitable as authoritative sources.

verification.observation:
The primary PDF URL resolves to a genuine, current FDOT document: a 20.8 MB, 1339-page PDF titled "Florida Department of Transportation Standard Specifications for Road and Bridge Construction, FY 2026-27," signed by the Director of Office of Design. Format claim (single-pdf) is correct — this is one consolidated PDF, and the specs library page confirms each edition is offered as Standard/Signed-and-Sealed/Redline eBooks, all single PDFs.

However, the numbering claim is materially wrong in two ways:

1. Division count/labeling: The document has only THREE divisions, labeled with Roman numerals — DIVISION I (General Requirements and Covenants, single/double-digit sections 1-9), DIVISION II (Construction Details, 3-digit sections 100-800s), DIVISION III (Materials, 3-digit sections 900s). There is no "Divisions 1-9" scheme — the claim appears to conflate Division I's section numbers (1-9) with a division count of nine. Only 3 divisions exist in this edition.

2. Section 105 example is incorrect: Section 105 is actually "Contractor Quality Control General Requirements," not "Control of Materials." (Control of Materials is Section 6, in Division I, using single-digit — not 3-digit — numbering.) The dashed/decimal subsection format itself is correct — Section 105 does contain subsection 105-8 "Personnel Qualifications" with further sub-numbering (105-8.1, 105-8.6, etc.) — but it's attached to the wrong section title in the claim.

Confirmed accurate: Section 916 "Bituminous Materials" does exist under Division III (Materials, 900s aggregate/flexible-pavement materials group), exactly as claimed. The History of Revisions page (fdot.gov/specifications/history-of-revisions) does group sections into ranges and literally shows a "Section 900-999" grouping as claimed, though the other ranges shown are "1-399," "400-599," "600-799" — not clean hundred-by-hundred groupings for the lower sections.

Net: the source and edition are real and correctly identified, and the dashed-subsection format plus the Section 916 and "Section 900-999" details check out. But the core numbering claim — "Divisions 1-9" and the "Section 105 (Control of Materials)" example — is factually wrong for this edition.

notes: IDOT's model is closer to a "base edition + annual supplement" structure than WSDOT's single-book-per-year model: a base Standard Specifications book (2012, 2016, or 2022) stays in force for years, and each January IDOT issues a Supplemental Specifications and Recurring Special Provisions document that amends/adds to it. For DOTcompass's history-across-editions feature this means the meaningful unit of comparison is likely (base edition + supplement year) pairs rather than one document per year, e.g. "2022 base + Jan 2023 supplement" vs "2022 base + Jan 2026 supplement" vs "2016 base." All documents are hosted on the state's PowerDMS instance (public.powerdms.com/IDOT) rather than on idot.illinois.gov directly; the idot.illinois.gov page is a landing/index page linking out to PowerDMS-hosted PDFs. No explicit copyright/reuse license statement was found on IDOT's site (unlike some states that post an explicit public-domain notice) — documents are freely downloadable without login, and IDOT will also mail complimentary hard copies to public officials, but silence on formal reuse terms means confidence on the "reuse" field specifically is medium-low even though confidence on the rest of the record is high. Section numbering is confirmed as decimal Article.Section style very similar to Washington's (e.g. Section 105 "Control of Work" contains subsections 105.01-105.19, such as 105.01 "Authority of the Engineer and Suspension of Work") — this should map cleanly onto DOTcompass's existing WSDOT-style section model. The primarySpecUrl given is the PowerDMS-hosted 2022 base document link, which was directly returned from IDOT's own standard-specifications landing page fetch, not invented.
numberingExample: Section 105.01 "Authority of the Engineer and Suspension of Work" (within Article/Section 105, "Control of Work") — decimal Article.Subsection format essentially parallel to WSDOT's 1-09.7 style, minus the leading division-number hyphen.
numberingScheme: Decimal section.subsection numbering (e.g., "105.01"), with sections grouped into broader numbered Articles/Divisions covering topics like General Provisions, Control of Work, Materials, Construction Details, and Measurement/Payment — same family of numbering as AASHTO-derived state specs (comparable in spirit to WSDOT's Division-Section.Subsection scheme, though IDOT does not use WSDOT's hyphenated division prefix).
reuse: No explicit copyright/reuse license statement was located on IDOT's official pages. Documents are freely downloadable as PDFs with no login/paywall; public officials/public bodies can also request complimentary printed copies via an order form, and others can purchase hard copies through IDOT's ePay site. As a work of Illinois state government, it likely is not subject to the assertion of copyright in the way private works are, but IDOT does not post an explicit public-domain/CC notice the way some agencies do — treat as "freely accessible, terms unstated" rather than "explicitly open licensed."

verification.observation:
The primary URL (https://www.in.gov/dot/div/contracts/standards/book/sep25/2026%20Standard%20Specifications.pdf) resolves with HTTP 200 and serves a real, genuine 1,328-page PDF titled "INDIANA DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION STANDARD SPECIFICATIONS 2026," dated/signed by actual INDOT officials (Commissioner Lyndsay Quist, Standards Committee members, etc.). No login wall, no redirect.

Numbering claim CONFIRMED: The document uses Division (3-digit: 100, 200...) > Section (3-digit, e.g. 101, 105, 111) > Subsection (2-digit decimal, e.g. 101.01, 105.03) numbering. Section 105.03 "Conformance with Plans and Specifications" exists exactly as claimed, appearing in the Table of Contents under "DIVISION 100 – GENERAL PROVISIONS / SECTION 105" at page 46, and is cross-referenced elsewhere in the text (e.g., page ~81: "...in accordance with 105.03"). This does mirror the AASHTO-derived Division.Section.Subsection style used by WSDOT, though WSDOT's own numbering (e.g. "1-09.7") is actually Division-Section hyphenated, not identical punctuation — INDOT's is period-separated three-digit-section style, a reasonable but not identical analogy.

Format claim CONTRADICTED: The claimed format is "per-division-pdf" (implying each division 100/200/.../900 is a separate PDF file). This is false for the given URL and current edition. The 2026 edition (like 2024, 2022, 2020) is published as a single combined PDF book containing all divisions in one 6.5MB, 1,328-page file — there is no per-division PDF split. Per-division/section-index style structure only applies to older editions (2018 and earlier) on the specs page, which are described as "combined PDF master books with separate index documents," not per-division PDFs either. So no version of INDOT's specs actually matches a "per-division-pdf" format as claimed — this appears to be a mischaracterization of the delivery format, though the content and section numbering itself are accurate.

Edition/date claim CONFIRMED: page 0 shows "2026" edition; specs page and letting notes confirm "Effective for Lettings on or after September 1, 2025 thru August 31, 2027" matching the claimed date range.

numberingScheme: Sections are numbered with 4-digit numbers grouped into thematic "Divisions" (e.g. 2000s = earthwork, 2100s = grading/excavation, 4100s = flexible pavement, etc.), with sub-numbering as SectionNumber.SubsectionNumber (e.g. 2102.01). This differs from WSDOT's Division-Section.Subsection (1-09.7) style; Iowa's Section numbers themselves encode the division (first digit(s)).

notes: Iowa DOT's primary current-content interface is the Electronic Reference Library (ERL) at ia.iowadot.gov/erl/current/GS/navigation/nav.htm, which presents each Section as its own HTML page (e.g. content/2102.htm, content/2107.htm) with hyperlinked cross-references, rather than a single static PDF — closer to WSDOT's HTML-per-section model than a monolithic PDF, though a full combined PDF (Standard_Specifications_2023.pdf, split into two volumes due to size) is also published for the base 2023 series text. The ERL is versioned by release (e.g. archiveoct2022, archiveoct2012 folder naming), and each semiannual release is archived at a stable URL under ia.iowadot.gov/erl/archiveXXXNNNN/GS/content/, which is exactly the kind of historical, section-addressable archive DOTcompass's history feature needs — this is a notably strong candidate for cross-edition section diffing, potentially better-suited to that feature than a PDF-only state. Because specs are amended via twice-yearly General Supplemental Specifications rather than a single monolithic new "edition," the concept of \"current edition\" is really the Series 2023 base text plus the latest GS overlay; treat both the archived-edition PDFs and the ERL's dated releases as valid history sources. Primary contact for specifications: dot.specifications@iowadot.us / 515-239-1742.

verification.observation:
The primary URL resolves fine: HTTP 200, content-type application/pdf, ~11.27MB, served from ia.iowadot.gov (last-modified Dec 8, 2025, indicating post-publication amendment). I downloaded and decoded the PDF text streams directly (no pdftotext available, so I parsed FlateDecode streams and Tj/TJ operators manually). Title page confirms: "STANDARD SPECIFICATIONS FOR HIGHWAY AND BRIDGE CONSTRUCTION SERIES 2023," Iowa DOT, Ames IA — this is authentic, not a redirect/404/paywall.

Format claim (per-division-pdf) is wrong in one respect: this primary URL is actually the FULL combined book (all divisions in one 11MB PDF, ~900+ pages), not split into separate per-division PDFs. Iowa DOT's site may also offer per-section/division PDFs elsewhere in the ERL, but the "primary spec URL" given is the monolithic single-file edition, contradicting "per-division-pdf" as the format of the cited URL.

Numbering/example claim: the SPECIFIC example is verified correct — Section 2102 is titled "Roadway and Borrow Excavation," subsection 2102.01 is literally labeled "DESCRIPTION," and Section 2107 is "Embankments" (confirmed in TOC: "2107. Embankments."). The SectionNumber.SubsectionNumber format (2102.01) is accurate.

However, the broader claimed division-grouping scheme is substantially wrong. The actual TOC shows Divisions are 2-digit numbers, not 4-digit "thousand-groupings": Division 11 (General Requirements), Division 20 (Equipment Requirements), Division 21 (Earthwork, Subgrades, and Subbases — sections 2101-2113+, this is a SINGLE division covering both "earthwork" and "grading/excavation," not two separate divisions/ranges as claimed), Division 22 (Base Courses), Division 23 (Surface Courses — this is where "2303. Flexible Pavement" actually lives), Division 24 (Structures), Division 25 (Misc. Construction), Division 26 (Roadside Development), Division 41 (Construction Materials, starting at section 4100 "General Provisions" — a materials-spec division, NOT flexible pavement). So the claim's parenthetical example "4100s = flexible pavement" is factually incorrect — flexible pavement is Section 2303 under Division 23, while Division 41/4100s is "Construction Materials" (aggregates, cement, steel, etc., referenced throughout other sections as "Apply Section 4XXX"). The "2000s = earthwork, 2100s = grading/excavation" framing is also a mischaracterization/invention — there's no such split; it's one Division 21 for all earthwork/subgrade/subbase work.

That page states: \"KDOT's standard specifications contain material, equipment, and construction requirements for items specified in the construction of the state transportation system. The standard specifications were updated in 2015,\" and offers three ways to view: (1) \"Download a PDF of the entire document\" (single combined PDF, link target: https://www.ksdot.gov/home/showpublisheddocument/5233/638941537050330000), (2) links to each individual Section as separate PDFs organized under Divisions 100 through 2600 (e.g. Division 100 = General Clauses and Covenants, containing Sections 101 Definitions and Terms, 102 Bidding Requirements, 103 Award and Execution of Contract, 104 Scope of Work, 105 Control of Work, 106 Control of Materials, 107 Legal Relations, 108 Prosecution and Progress, etc.; Division 1100 = Aggregates; Division 1200 = Asphalt Materials), and (3) a request form to order a printed hard copy (https://kdotapp.ksdot.gov/StdSpecsConsManForm/default.aspx). Numbering scheme: Section numbers like \"105\" (Control of Work) map directly to per-section PDFs; individual special-provision/errata documents carry codes like \"15-01019-R02\" (year-prefix + item number + revision suffix) layered on top of the base section text - this is Kansas's mechanism for incremental updates between full-book editions, functionally similar to how Washington issues numbered \"General Special Provisions\" (GSPs) atop its 1-xx.x sections. An Errata sheet (\"15-ER-1-R23\") and a combined \"Table of Contents and Subject Index\" PDF are also published. No stand-alone archive of prior complete editions (e.g. 1990, 2007) was located on ksdot.gov; those older editions are known to exist only via third-party mirrors (e.g. a scanned 2007 Bridge Design Manual on tsp2.org, a 2014 Bridge Design Manual on Scribd) - not an official state-hosted historical archive. This is the key gap versus Washington's DOTcompass model: Kansas maintains ONE current base edition updated via layered special-provisions/errata PDFs rather than publishing successive dated whole-book editions online, so a "diff across editions" UI would need to be redesigned around date-stamped special-provision revisions to individual items/sections rather than edition-to-edition section comparison. No explicit copyright/reuse statement was found on the specifications page itself; the site is a standard .gov page with the usual implied public-domain-ish status of state government work product, but no license or reuse policy language was located in the fetched content, so this should be treated as unconfirmed rather than assumed permissive.

verification.observation:
The primary spec URL resolves cleanly (HTTP 200, application/pdf, 5.75MB, last-modified June 2026, no login/redirect). Downloaded and text-extracted the 944-page PDF directly (poppler unavailable, used pypdf).

Confirmed details:
- Cover page: fill color RGB(0.311,0.149,0.513) = purple background, "Department of Transportation" rendered in white (1,1,1 rg), "Standard" rendered in gold (1,0.776,0.185 rg), title "March 2026" / "STATE OF MAINE" — matches the claimed purple cover with white/gold lettering.
- Divisions confirmed present exactly as claimed: Division 100 General Conditions, Division 300 Bases, Division 400 Pavements, Division 500 Structures, Division 600 Miscellaneous Construction, Division 700 Materials (200s Earthwork also present, per section 203 found at "2-15" pagination).
- Numbering scheme confirmed: single-dot Section.Subsection format, e.g. "SECTION 401 - HOT MIX ASPHALT PAVEMENT" with "401.01 Description," "401.02 Materials" exactly as claimed. Section 203 - "EXCAVATION AND EMBANKMENT" with "203.01 Description" and "203.02 Materials" confirmed.

One notable inaccuracy in the researcher's illustrative example: "Muck Excavation and Waste Disposal" is NOT a subsection title under 203.01/203.02 in the Standard Specifications — 203.01 is "Description" and 203.02 is "Materials". "Muck Excavation And Waste Disposal" is actually the title of a separate Standard DETAIL numbered "203(01)" (a drawing/detail document, not a spec subsection), referenced within the text of Section 203. So the example subsection content the researcher cited is misattributed, though the general numbering pattern (Section.Subsection, e.g. 401.02) is correct and well-supported by direct examples (401.01/401.02, 203.01/203.02).

Also noted (not the researcher's error, but a document-internal quirk): the body text on page 9 (Section 100.1) literally says "This 2025 edition of the Maine Department of Transportation's Standard Specifications..." despite the cover reading "March 2026" — an apparent leftover drafting artifact in MaineDOT's own document, unrelated to the claim being verified.

No login wall, no redirect, no 404 — the URL is live and serves the real, current document.

notes: IMPORTANT CAVEAT: The NDOT Standard Specifications appear stale/thin compared to WSDOT. The live specs-and-plans page (https://www.dot.nv.gov/doing-business/contractors-construction/contract-services/standard-specifications-and-plans, formerly at .../public-involvement-information/transportation-planning/standard-specifications-and-plans) lists only TWO editions of the Standard Specifications ever: 2001 and 2014, with 2014 still marked "CURRENT" as of an April 2026 site snapshot — i.e., NDOT has not issued a new edition of the Specifications in 12 years. Oddly, the cover-image alt text on that page reads "Cover of the 2024 NDOT Standard Specifications for Road and Bridge Construction," but the actual link, filename, and document ID are unchanged and still point to the 2014 PDF — this looks like a CMS content-editing artifact (possibly a 2024 edition in progress whose alt-text was updated prematurely) rather than an actual new 2024 release; I could not find any live, distinct 2024 Standard Specifications PDF. This directly affects DOTcompass's flagship "section history across editions" feature: NDOT provides only 2 data points (2001, 2014) for the Specifications, versus presumably many more for WSDOT. Do NOT confuse this with the "Standard Plans for Road and Bridge Construction" (a separate, drawings-only document), which DOES have a rich edition archive back to 1974 (1974,1975,1976,1980,1983,1986,1987,1988,1989,1991,1993,1994,1995,1996,1997,1999,2001,2003,2005,2007,2010,2017,2020,2022,2024) — that archive is real and deep, just not for the prose Specifications. Format: 2014 Specifications is one single PDF (610 pages, Acrobat, page-locked/encrypted but text-extractable), organized into Divisions (e.g. "DIVISION I — GENERAL REQUIREMENTS") each containing 3-digit numbered Sections, with decimal subsections — e.g. Section 105 "Control of Work," subsection 105.05 "Partnering," 105.09 "Construction Stakes, Lines, and Grades" — i.e. WSDOT-style "Section 105.05" numbering, not Caltrans 6-digit item numbers. No explicit copyright/reuse license was found; only a disclaimer stating the standards are intended for NDOT's own design/construction use and that recipients are responsible for authenticating standards before use elsewhere — silence on reuse rights, so treat as "all rights reserved absent a stated license" for tool purposes. dot.nv.gov blocks direct scripted fetches (403 for curl/WebFetch even with browser UA) — verification here relied on Internet Archive Wayback snapshots (200 OK, Nov 2025 and Apr 2026) of the official page, plus a working mirror PDF at a Nevada State Library domain (epubs.nsla.nv.gov, also .gov) which I fetched and inspected directly to confirm the 2014 edition's content/structure. Recommend a human double-check with NDOT's Specifications Engineer (contact on the page: currently listed as Ryan Bennett, Specifications Supervisor, (775) 888-7607, RBennett@dot.nv.gov) on whether a newer edition is imminent, since the site itself is not confirmed live-fetchable by this agent.

notes: Key finding for DOTcompass: NJDOT's specification-history model differs structurally from WSDOT's. Instead of publishing a clean sequence of dated whole-book editions, NJDOT keeps ONE current base edition (2019, itself an update of 2007) that is perpetually patched via numbered "Baseline Document Changes" (BDCs) — over 80 since 2019 — each a standalone PDF amending specific sections/pages. A "history across editions" feature for NJ would need to be built as a diff across BDCs layered onto the 2019 base text, not as a simple archive of dated PDFs like WA. Only two full base editions (2007, 2019) are on the DOT's own site; earlier editions (e.g. 1961) exist only as scans in the New Jersey State Library digital repository (dspace.njstatelib.org), an external, non-DOT source. Section numbering is AASHTO-standard Division/Section/Subsection format (e.g., Division 100 General Provisions, Section 105 Control of Work, subsections like 105.01), which is close to Washington's format but without WA's hyphenated "1-09.7" pagination convention. Direct PDF link for current spec (with BDCs through the date shown on the page) is https://dot.nj.gov/transportation/eng/specs/2019/pdf/StandSpecRoadBridge%202019%2020260610.pdf — confirmed via the specs page listing, though the DOT domain returned a 403 to a plain curl request (likely bot/WAF protection, e.g. Incapsula) so treat as "referenced on the official page" rather than fully verified by direct fetch. Also relevant: an "Original" (unmodified 2019 baseline, pre-BDC) PDF is separately posted, which is useful for reconstructing edition history.

verification.observation:
The PDF URL resolves (HTTP 200) to a real, genuine SDDOT document: "STANDARD SPECIFICATIONS FOR ROADS AND BRIDGES, Version: 10-1-2025, SOUTH DAKOTA DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION," 596 pages, single PDF (~8.4MB). The edition/version claim is confirmed exactly as stated.

However, the numbering claim is materially wrong:
- Division I - General Provisions is NOT a "100-series" section range. Per the table of contents and body text, Division I sections are numbered 1 through 9 (e.g., "1 DEFINITION AND TERMS," "2 BIDDING REQUIREMENTS AND CONDITIONS," ... "5 CONTROL OF WORK," "9 MEASUREMENT AND PAYMENT"). Control of Work is Section 5, not Section 105. There is no "105.01" anywhere; the actual subsection numbering for Control of Work is 5.1, 5.2 ... 5.16 (confirmed by reading the actual body text, e.g. "5.13 MAINTENANCE OF PROJECT," "5.16 PROJECT ACCEPTANCE").
- The decimal subsection format is also misdescribed. It's single-digit decimal (X.Y), not double-digit (X.YY) as the claim's example "105.01, 105.02" implies. Confirmed in Division II as well: Section 260 (Granular Bases and Surfacing) uses "260.1 DESCRIPTION," "260.2 MATERIALS," "260.3 CONSTRUCTION REQUIREMENTS" — not "260.01."
- Division II - Construction Details section range/examples ARE correct: TOC shows "100 – 740 DIVISION II - CONSTRUCTION DETAILS," and both named examples check out verbatim: "260 GRANULAR BASES AND SURFACING" (page 148) and "320 ASPHALT CONCRETE, GENERAL" appears in the TOC.

So: format claim (single-pdf) — correct. Edition claim — correct. Division II section numbers/examples — correct. But the core numbering description for Division I (claimed 100-series, with 105 Control of Work / 105.01 subsections) is fabricated/incorrect — Division I actually uses single-digit section numbers 1-9 with single-decimal subsections (5.1, 5.16, etc).

verification.observation:
The primary URL (Google Drive folder) does resolve and is a real, publicly-shared UDOT-related folder titled "2027 Standards Books Folder," containing exactly two files: "2027 Standard Drawings_Official.pdf" (69.7MB) and "2027 Standard Specifications_Official.pdf" (6.9MB). The UDOT specs page (connect.udot.utah.gov/business/standards/) confirms the edition-cadence claim reasonably well: it lists 2025, 2026, and 2027 Standard Specification/Drawing book sets concurrently available, with a note that previous versions require a GRAMA request — consistent with an annual (rather than 4-year) release cycle, each edition tied to a construction year.

However, the numbering-scheme claim is unsupported and likely wrong. Nothing in the fetched pages shows AASHTO/FP-style three-digit "107"/"107.01" numbering. A web search instead turned up a real UDOT document titled "Supplemental Specification 2008 Standard Specification Book SECTION 00120M" — indicating UDOT actually uses a five-digit CSI-MasterFormat-style section numbering (e.g., 00120, 01035), not the three-digit "Section 107.01" AASHTO style used by WSDOT-family states as claimed. Net: URL is genuine and live, edition-availability claim holds up, but the claimed file format (per-division PDFs) is contradicted by direct observation (single consolidated PDF per book), and the claimed section-numbering scheme (107.01-style) is unsupported/likely incorrect for UDOT, which appears to use CSI-style 5-digit numbering instead.

Also, 107.10 does exist, but it is titled "Use of Explosives," a subsection under the Section 107 umbrella — the claim's parenthetical "(Legal Relations and Responsibility to the Public)" attached to "107.10" is misleadingly worded (that's the parent Section 107 title, not the 107.10 subsection title), and combined with the fabricated 105.03 example, suggests the numbering example was not actually checked against the source document.

numberingScheme: Organized into numbered Divisions (100s General Provisions, 200s Earthwork, 300s Aggregate Materials, 400s Pavements, 500s Structures, 600s Miscellaneous Construction, 700s Traffic/Electrical, 800s Structures/Reinforcing, etc.), each containing numbered Sections (e.g. Section 105, Section 401), broken into decimal Subsections (Section.Subsection, e.g. 105.9) and further into sub-subsections (e.g. 401.3.3.3) — directly analogous to WSDOT's Division/Section/Subsection numbering, differing mainly in that Wyoming numbers full sections 3-digit (1xx-8xx) rather than WSDOT's "1-09.7" hyphenated style.
numberingExample: Section 109.7, Mobilization (within Division 100, General Provisions); also Section 401.3.3.3, Drum Plants (within Division 400, Pavements)
currentEdition: 2021 Edition (adopted by the Transportation Commission of Wyoming, August 20, 2020)
historicalDepth: Confirmed two editions hosted on WYDOT's own domain: 2021 (current) and 2010 (still live at a WYDOT URL, https://www.dot.state.wy.us/files/live/sites/wydot/files/shared/Construction/2010%20Standard%20Specifications/2010%20Standard%20Specifications.pdf). The current Standard Specifications landing page itself only prominently links the 2021 edition plus its 2021 Supplemental Specifications (hosted in a Google Drive folder); no visible archive index of multiple prior editions (1990s/2000s) was found via search or on the page. So confirmed depth is "current (2021) + prior (2010)," i.e. ~11 years / one prior edition confirmed; older editions may exist but were not found indexed or linked.

Manuals and Publications (index page for the above traffic/safety manuals — individual manuals are linked as direct PDF downloads rather than dedicated sub-pages)

reuse: No explicit copyright/reuse notice was found within the Standard Specifications PDF itself (front matter contains only acknowledgements, no © statement). However, WYDOT's related Standard Plans page requires users to accept click-through terms stating the Standard Plans "are copyrighted by the Wyoming Department of Transportation," disclaiming warranty/accuracy, requiring the user to indemnify WYDOT, prohibiting resale/advertising of the files, restricting redistribution to the format provided, and stating the printed copy controls in case of conflict with the electronic version. This suggests WYDOT asserts copyright over its engineering documents generally, even though the Standard Specifications PDF itself doesn't carry an explicit notice.
notes: Primary spec PDF URL was directly fetched and its content verified (771 pages, title page confirms "Standard Specifications for Road and Bridge Construction, Wyoming Department of Transportation, 2021 Edition," adopted August 20, 2020). Format is a single large PDF (not per-division). A 2021 Supplemental Specifications document also exists but is hosted in a Google Drive folder (linked from the Standard Specifications page) rather than on the WYDOT domain — noteworthy for the tool since supplements may not be as reliably archivable/scrapeable as the main PDF. Historical-archive depth is the weakest point of confidence: only two editions (2021, 2010) were confirmed as currently live WYDOT URLs; no clear public index/archive page listing all historical editions (unlike WSDOT's dedicated history archive) was located — this differs materially from Washington's setup and may limit the flagship 'history across editions' feature for Wyoming unless older editions are found via other means (e.g. Wayback Machine, State Library, or direct request to WYDOT). The Bridge Design Manual is split into many per-chapter PDFs each with independent revision dates (e.g. Section 1.02 revised April 2024, Chapter 3 revised January 2024), which is a different format from the single-PDF Standard Specifications and would need separate handling in DOTcompass.

verification.observation: Fetched the primary spec URL directly: HTTP 200, Content-Type application/pdf, 7,013,517 bytes (~6.7MB), no login/redirect required — confirms single-pdf format. Downloaded and parsed the PDF (771 pages) with pdfplumber. Page 1 reads "Standard Specifications for Road and Bridge Construction, 2021 Edition, Adopted by the Transportation Commission of Wyoming, August 20, 2020" — exactly matching the claimed edition/adoption date. The Table of Contents (pages 5-10 of the PDF) confirms the Division structure exactly as claimed: DIVISION 100 General Provisions (Sections 101-114), DIVISION 200 Earthwork (201-221), DIVISION 300 Aggregate Materials (301-310), DIVISION 400 Pavements (401-418), DIVISION 500 Structures (501-516), DIVISION 600 Miscellaneous Construction (601+, seen through 610 Metal Drain Inlets) — consistent with numbered 100s-800s divisions containing 3-digit sections. Verified both example citations directly in body text: Section 109.7 "Mobilization" appears in the TOC under SECTION 109 Measurement and Payment (Division 100), page 106 — exact match. Section 401.3.3.3 "Drum Plants" appears verbatim as a subheading within SECTION 401 Plant Mix Pavements and Recycled Plant Mix Pavements (Division 400 Pavements), with surrounding subsections 401.3.4, 401.3.4.1, 401.3.4.2, 401.3.5 confirming the decimal sub-subsection numbering pattern (Section.Subsection.sub-subsection.sub-sub-subsection) exactly as described. No discrepancies found in format, numbering scheme, edition, or adoption date. The specs page itself (fetched separately) confirms this PDF is the listed 2021 Standard Specifications document, plus separate 2021 Supplemental Specifications on Google Drive (not part of the claim, so not an issue).
